items.q: What is the difference between profit and revenue?
items.correct: Revenue is total income from sales (price x quantity sold); profit is revenue minus total costs — what is left after paying for everything.
items.misconception: Profit and revenue are different words for the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Profit is what businesses earn; revenue is what employees earn.
items.why: A business can have huge revenue but make no profit if costs are equally huge. Conflating them leads to nonsensical analysis — Amazon had high revenue but low profits for years.

items.q: What is the difference between fixed and variable costs?
items.correct: Fixed costs do not change with output level (e.g. rent, salaries); variable costs change in proportion to output (e.g. raw materials, hourly wages).
items.misconception: Fixed costs are paid every month and variable costs are paid weekly.
items.random_wrong: Fixed costs are the cheapest costs and variable costs are the most expensive.
items.why: The distinction is about behaviour with output, not payment frequency. Rent is fixed because producing more units doesn't increase it; flour for a bakery is variable because more loaves means more flour.

items.q: What are the four Ps of the marketing mix?
items.correct: Product, Price, Place (distribution), and Promotion — the controllable variables a business uses to satisfy customers and achieve objectives.
items.misconception: The four Ps are Product, Price, Profit, and People.
items.random_wrong: The four Ps are Planning, Producing, Promoting, and Profit.
items.why: Students often substitute 'profit' or 'people' into the four Ps. The classic McCarthy framework is Product, Price, Place and Promotion — extended models add others but those four are foundational.

items.q: What does Maslow's hierarchy of needs propose about motivation?
items.correct: Humans are motivated by a hierarchy of needs (physiological, safety, social, esteem, self-actualisation) — lower needs must be substantially met before higher ones motivate.
items.misconception: Maslow said money is the most important motivator at work.
items.random_wrong: Maslow said employees must be promoted every year.
items.why: Students conflate Maslow with Taylor (who DID emphasise pay). Maslow's whole point is that once basic needs are met, higher needs (recognition, self-actualisation) motivate — not more money.

items.q: What is Herzberg's distinction between hygiene factors and motivators?
items.correct: Hygiene factors (pay, conditions, policies) prevent dissatisfaction when present but don't motivate; motivators (recognition, achievement, responsibility) create positive satisfaction.
items.misconception: Higher pay always motivates employees.
items.random_wrong: Hygiene factors are about workplace cleanliness.
items.why: Pay is a hygiene factor — its absence demotivates but its presence doesn't motivate beyond a point. Students treat pay as the prime motivator; Herzberg's theory directly challenges this.

items.q: What is Taylor's theory of scientific management?
items.correct: Workers are motivated primarily by money; efficiency is maximised by breaking tasks into standardised steps, training workers in 'the one best way', and using piece-rate pay.
items.misconception: Taylor said workers are motivated by recognition and teamwork.
items.random_wrong: Taylor invented the assembly line at Ford.
items.why: Students confuse Taylor with Mayo or Maslow. Taylor's view was that workers respond to pay incentives, not social or psychological factors — his theory is famously narrow and mechanistic.

items.q: When might an autocratic leadership style be appropriate?
items.correct: In crises requiring quick decisions, when workers are inexperienced, or in highly hierarchical contexts — though it can damage morale long-term.
items.misconception: Autocratic leadership is always wrong and democratic leadership is always right.
items.random_wrong: Autocratic leaders are dictators who break the law.
items.why: Students moralise leadership styles. The business studies view is contingency-based — each style has appropriate contexts. Democratic is not universally best; autocracy works in emergencies.

items.q: What is the difference between a stakeholder and a shareholder?
items.correct: A shareholder owns shares in the company; a stakeholder is anyone affected by or with interest in the business — employees, customers, suppliers, community, plus shareholders.
items.misconception: Stakeholders and shareholders are different spellings of the same word.
items.random_wrong: Stakeholders are managers and shareholders are workers.
items.why: All shareholders are stakeholders, but most stakeholders are not shareholders. Students treat the terms as interchangeable, missing the wider scope of stakeholder analysis vs ownership.

items.q: Why can a profitable business still fail?
items.correct: Because cash flow and profit are different — a business may be profitable on paper but run out of cash to pay bills, especially if customers owe money or stock ties up funds.
items.misconception: If a business is profitable it cannot run out of money.
items.random_wrong: Profitable businesses fail only when employees steal.
items.why: Cash-flow problems sink profitable companies routinely. Profit is accounting; cash is liquidity. Students treat the two as equivalent and miss why businesses go bust despite paper profits.

items.q: What is limited liability?
items.correct: Shareholders' personal assets are protected if the company fails — they can only lose the amount they invested, not their personal wealth.
items.misconception: Limited liability means the company has limited debts it can take on.
items.random_wrong: Limited liability means a business can only operate for a limited time.
items.why: Limited liability is about the LIMIT on what shareholders can lose, not on what the company can do. Students misread the term as restricting the business rather than protecting investors.

items.q: What is the difference between primary and secondary market research?
items.correct: Primary research is original data the business gathers itself (surveys, interviews); secondary research uses existing data (reports, government statistics, market intelligence).
items.misconception: Primary research is the most important and secondary research is less important.
items.random_wrong: Primary research is done first and secondary research is done later.
items.why: Primary and secondary refer to data SOURCE, not importance or order. Secondary research is often done first (it's cheaper). Both are valuable for different reasons.

items.q: What are economies of scale?
items.correct: Cost advantages a firm gains as it grows — average cost per unit falls because fixed costs spread over more units, bulk discounts, and specialisation become possible.
items.misconception: Economies of scale means total costs decrease as the business grows.
items.random_wrong: Economies of scale means a business saves the economy money.
items.why: Total costs RISE with growth — it's AVERAGE cost per unit that falls. Students confuse total and average, missing the precise meaning of the concept.

items.q: What does PESTLE analysis assess?
items.correct: The external macro-environment: Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal and Environmental factors affecting a business.
items.misconception: PESTLE analyses the internal strengths and weaknesses of a business.
items.random_wrong: PESTLE stands for Profit, Earnings, Sales, Trade, Loans, Equity.
items.why: Students confuse PESTLE (external) with SWOT (internal + external). PESTLE is about the wider environment a business cannot control; SWOT mixes internal and external.

items.q: What is a brand in marketing?
items.correct: A combination of name, design, symbols and associations that distinguishes a product and creates customer perception and loyalty — more than just a logo.
items.misconception: A brand is just the logo on a product.
items.random_wrong: A brand is the company that makes a product.
items.why: Branding is the sum of perceptions, not just visual identity. Coca-Cola the brand is much more than the script logo — it is associations, taste expectations, advertising history.

items.q: What four growth strategies does Ansoff's Matrix identify?
items.correct: Market penetration (existing product, existing market), market development (existing product, new market), product development (new product, existing market), diversification (new product, new market).
items.misconception: Ansoff's Matrix is about analysing competitors.
items.random_wrong: Ansoff's Matrix shows market share against profit.
items.why: Students confuse Ansoff (growth strategy) with Porter's Five Forces (competition) or BCG Matrix (portfolio). Ansoff is specifically about the four product/market combinations for growth.

items.q: What is a franchise?
items.correct: An arrangement where a franchisor grants a franchisee the right to use their brand, products and systems in exchange for fees and a share of revenue.
items.misconception: A franchise is when a business owns multiple branches itself.
items.random_wrong: A franchise is when employees buy shares in their company.
items.why: Owned chains are not franchises. A franchise involves separate ownership — McDonald's restaurants are mostly franchised (owned by individual operators), not owned by McDonald's Corporation.

items.q: Why does a company's share price change?
items.correct: Share prices reflect investors' expectations about future profits — driven by company performance, sector trends, economic conditions, and market sentiment.
items.misconception: Share prices go up when a company makes more money this year.
items.random_wrong: Share prices are set by the government each morning.
items.why: Markets are forward-looking. A company can report record profits and see shares fall if results were worse than expected. Students miss the expectations-driven nature of pricing.

items.q: What is productivity?
items.correct: Output per unit of input (per worker, per hour) — measuring efficiency, not total output. A small efficient firm can be more productive than a large inefficient one.
items.misconception: Productivity is the total amount produced by a business.
items.random_wrong: Productivity is the number of employees a business has.
items.why: Total output and productivity are different. A factory of 100 workers producing 1000 units is less productive than 10 workers producing 200 units. The ratio matters, not the total.

items.q: What is the purpose of a mission statement?
items.correct: To define the business's core purpose and values, guiding decisions and communicating identity to stakeholders — answering 'why we exist'.
items.misconception: A mission statement is a list of financial targets.
items.random_wrong: A mission statement is a description of what products the business sells.
items.why: Mission statements are qualitative and values-driven, not numerical or product-list. Students confuse mission (purpose) with objectives (specific targets) or product range.

items.q: Why might a business adopt corporate social responsibility (CSR)?
items.correct: Reasons include genuine ethics, but also commercial benefits — improved reputation, customer loyalty, employee retention, and reduced regulatory or reputational risk.
items.misconception: CSR is purely about being ethical with no business benefit.
items.random_wrong: CSR is the legal requirement to pay taxes.
items.why: CSR is both ethical and strategic. Students often treat it as either pure morality or pure cynicism — in business studies the balanced answer recognises both motivations.

items.q: What is just-in-time (JIT) stock management?
items.correct: Holding minimal stock and receiving inputs only when needed for production — reducing storage costs but increasing vulnerability to supply disruption.
items.misconception: Just-in-time means delivering products to customers exactly on time.
items.random_wrong: Just-in-time means finishing work right before the deadline.
items.why: JIT is about INPUT stock to production, not output delivery to customers. Students misread the term as a delivery promise; it is an internal stock control philosophy.

items.q: What do McGregor's Theory X and Theory Y describe?
items.correct: Two contrasting management assumptions: Theory X assumes workers dislike work and need control; Theory Y assumes workers can be self-motivated and seek responsibility.
items.misconception: Theory X is about working in a factory and Theory Y is about office work.
items.random_wrong: Theory X and Theory Y are stages of business growth.
items.why: The theories are about MANAGER assumptions, not industry or growth stages. McGregor's point is that managers' beliefs about workers shape how they manage and the outcomes they get.

items.q: Which sector does manufacturing belong to?
items.correct: The secondary sector — which transforms raw materials from the primary sector (farming, mining) into finished goods.
items.misconception: Manufacturing is in the primary sector because it makes things.
items.random_wrong: Manufacturing is in the tertiary sector along with services.
items.why: Primary = extracting raw materials; secondary = transforming them; tertiary = services. 'Making things' suggests primary to students, but only extraction is primary — building/processing is secondary.

items.q: What are Porter's two main generic strategies for competitive advantage?
items.correct: Cost leadership (being the lowest-cost producer) and differentiation (offering unique value customers will pay more for) — Porter argues firms should choose one or the other.
items.misconception: Porter says businesses should pursue both low cost and differentiation at once.
items.random_wrong: Porter's strategies are about marketing channels.
items.why: Porter's central warning is against being 'stuck in the middle'. Students assume more strategies = better; Porter argues choosing one focus is more effective than trying both.

items.q: What is a key disadvantage of being a sole trader?
items.correct: Unlimited liability — the owner's personal assets are at risk if the business fails, because there is no legal separation between owner and business.
items.misconception: The main disadvantage is that sole traders can't sell any products.
items.random_wrong: Sole traders must employ at least one other person by law.
items.why: Sole traders can sell products and don't need employees. The key legal disadvantage is unlimited liability — the personal risk exposure, which is why many businesses become limited companies as they grow.

items.q: What three Ps are added to make the extended marketing mix (7Ps)?
items.correct: People (staff/service quality), Process (how the service is delivered), and Physical Evidence (tangible cues — uniforms, packaging, branding) — added to make the 4Ps relevant to services marketing.
items.misconception: The 7Ps are just the 4Ps repeated with different words.
items.random_wrong: The 7Ps include Profit, Planning and Performance.
items.why: The extended mix specifically addresses services, where customer experience depends on staff, process and atmospherics — not just product. Students treat 4Ps and 7Ps as interchangeable, missing what services marketing requires.

items.q: How does HRM differ from traditional personnel management?
items.correct: HRM treats people as a strategic asset and integrates human resource policies with business strategy; personnel management is more administrative and reactive, handling hiring, pay and disputes.
items.misconception: HRM and personnel are exactly the same thing — just a name change.
items.random_wrong: HRM only applies to large multinational companies.
items.why: The shift from personnel to HRM is conceptual — from administrative function to strategic partner. Students treat the relabel as cosmetic, missing the strategic management literature behind it.

items.q: What did Mayo's Hawthorne studies suggest about motivation?
items.correct: Workers are motivated by social factors and the feeling of being noticed/valued — not just pay or physical conditions. Productivity rose because researchers paid attention to workers, not because lighting improved.
items.misconception: Mayo proved that better lighting increases productivity.
items.random_wrong: Mayo invented the assembly line.
items.why: The Hawthorne effect is famously about attention and social meaning, not the experimental variable itself. Students remember the lighting experiment and miss the human relations conclusion Mayo drew from it.

items.q: When can a laissez-faire leadership style be effective?
items.correct: When employees are highly skilled, self-motivated and need autonomy — common in creative or expert teams. It can fail with inexperienced staff or in crises requiring direction.
items.misconception: Laissez-faire leadership means the leader doesn't care about the business.
items.random_wrong: Laissez-faire leadership is illegal under employment law.
items.why: Laissez-faire is a deliberate choice to delegate, not negligence. Students moralise it as lazy management, missing its appropriateness for skilled autonomous workers and innovation-led settings.

items.q: What is the difference between gross profit and net profit?
items.correct: Gross profit = revenue minus cost of goods sold (direct costs only); net profit = revenue minus ALL costs including overheads, interest and tax. Net profit is the bottom line.
items.misconception: Gross profit and net profit are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Gross profit is profit before VAT and net profit is profit after VAT.
items.why: The gap between gross and net reveals overhead burden. A high gross but low net margin means heavy fixed costs or financing. Students conflate the two and misread profitability.

items.q: How is Return on Capital Employed (ROCE) calculated?
items.correct: Operating profit divided by capital employed (total equity + non-current liabilities), expressed as a percentage. It measures how efficiently the business uses its long-term financing to generate profit.
items.misconception: ROCE is just net profit divided by revenue.
items.random_wrong: ROCE is the wages divided by hours worked.
items.why: ROCE compares profit to capital used, not revenue. It distinguishes capital-efficient businesses from revenue-heavy but capital-intensive ones. Students often confuse it with profit margin.

items.q: What does break-even output represent?
items.correct: The level of output where total revenue equals total costs — neither profit nor loss. It is NOT the level where profit is maximised; profit grows beyond break-even.
items.misconception: Break-even is where the business makes the most profit.
items.random_wrong: Break-even is where the business owes no debt.
items.why: Break-even is a no-profit-no-loss point, useful for risk analysis. Students conflate it with profit maximisation. Going above break-even is where profit starts; below break-even means a loss.

items.q: What does a liquidity ratio like the current ratio measure?
items.correct: A business's ability to meet short-term obligations — current assets divided by current liabilities. A ratio above 1 means assets cover liabilities; too high may indicate poor use of resources.
items.misconception: A higher liquidity ratio is always better.
items.random_wrong: Liquidity ratios measure how quickly a business sells products.
items.why: Very high ratios can signal idle cash or excessive stock — capital that could earn returns. Liquidity is about meeting bills on time, not maximising assets. Students assume bigger is better and miss the trade-off.

items.q: What are Porter's Five Forces?
items.correct: Competitive rivalry, threat of new entrants, threat of substitutes, bargaining power of suppliers, bargaining power of buyers — together determining industry attractiveness and potential profitability.
items.misconception: Porter's Five Forces are five business strategies a firm should choose between.
items.random_wrong: Porter's Five Forces are five physical security measures for a factory.
items.why: Five Forces analyse INDUSTRY structure, not firm strategy choices (that's Porter's generic strategies, a different framework). Students conflate the two Porter models.

items.q: What is corporate governance, and how does it differ from CSR?
items.correct: Corporate governance is the system of rules, practices and processes by which a company is directed and controlled — focusing on accountability, board structure and shareholder rights. CSR is about wider social/environmental responsibility.
items.misconception: Corporate governance is the same as corporate social responsibility.
items.random_wrong: Corporate governance is the legal requirement to have a CEO.
items.why: Governance is about how the company is run (board, audit, transparency); CSR is about what the company does for society. Students conflate them — both concern ethics but at different levels.

items.q: Why are hygiene factors and motivators not on the same scale in Herzberg's theory?
items.correct: Hygiene factors only prevent dissatisfaction (their absence demotivates, presence is neutral); motivators create satisfaction (their presence motivates, absence is neutral). They affect different outcomes, not different points on one scale.
items.misconception: Hygiene factors and motivators are just two types of motivation that combine.
items.random_wrong: Hygiene factors are about workplace cleanliness only.
items.why: Herzberg's key insight is dual-factor: satisfaction and dissatisfaction are not opposites but separate dimensions. Students collapse them to a single motivation scale, missing the structural claim.

items.q: Why are cash flow and profit different on different time-scales?
items.correct: Profit is recognised when a sale is made (even on credit); cash flow is when money actually moves. A profitable sale today may produce no cash for 60 days while bills are due — creating a cash-flow problem despite profit.
items.misconception: Cash flow and profit are the same thing measured differently.
items.random_wrong: Cash flow only matters at year-end.
items.why: The timing gap between recognition and receipt is what sinks profitable companies. Students conflate the two accounting concepts, missing why working capital management is critical.

items.q: What is operations management concerned with?
items.correct: The design and control of the production process and business operations — converting inputs into outputs efficiently. It includes capacity, quality, supply chain, and process improvement.
items.misconception: Operations management is the same as general management.
items.random_wrong: Operations management is only about manufacturing factories.
items.why: Operations is a specialised function focused on the production process; it applies equally to services (hospitals, airlines). Students treat it as factory-only or as general management, missing the distinctive process-focused discipline.

items.q: How do stakeholder and shareholder interests overlap and conflict?
items.correct: Shareholders are a subset of stakeholders. Their interests overlap (long-term success benefits all) but conflict on issues like dividends vs investment, wages vs profits, or short-term gains vs long-term sustainability.
items.misconception: Stakeholders and shareholders always want the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Shareholders are external and stakeholders are internal.
items.why: The shareholder-stakeholder distinction is central to debates on corporate purpose. Students miss the structural tension — a board can't always please everyone simultaneously, which is what stakeholder analysis exists to examine.
